content.claim: SNI classification in Hungary functions primarily as a financing and service-eligibility category rather than strictly clinical diagnosis. Until 2013, institutions educating SNI students received higher per-capita normative state funding, creating potential institutional incentives to identify/register students; conversely, more recent reports suggest SNI status increasingly functions as an exclusionary rather than inclusive factor in admissions as institutional financing fails to support extra resources.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Current normatíva regulation texts were not consulted to verify whether historical funding incentives still apply
the claim about SNI shifting from advantage to exclusion is sourced to one pedagogical journal and not independently verified against institution-level admissions data
direction of incentive effects (inclusion vs. exclusion) may vary by region, institution type, and BTMN severity category
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
